sections.title: Hypothetical: -(으)ㄴ/는다면
sections.explanation: -(으)ㄴ/는다면 is a stronger hypothetical conditional meaning 'if (hypothetically)' — it emphasizes that the condition is imagined, unlikely, or being considered as a possibility. It combines the plain-style ending with -면. Action verbs present: -ㄴ다면 (vowel stem) / -는다면 (consonant stem). 간다면 (if [one] goes), 먹는다면 (if [one] eats). Descriptive verbs: -다면. 좋다면 (if it's good), 크다면 (if it's big). Past: -았/었다면. 갔다면 (if [one] had gone). This pattern is common for wishful thinking, philosophical questions, and discussing unlikely scenarios. Compare: 비가 오면 (if it rains — neutral, might happen) vs. 비가 온다면 (if it were to rain — more hypothetical emphasis).
sections.tip: -(으)면 is a neutral 'if' for everyday conditions. -(으)ㄴ/는다면 adds a layer of 'suppose/imagine that...' — use it for thought experiments, wishes, and unlikely conditions.

examples.ko: 만약 로또에 당첨된다면 뭐 할 거예요?
examples.en: If you hypothetically won the lottery, what would you do?
If I had time, I'd want to travel
If I were a bird, I would fly in the sky

examples.ko: 다시 학생이 된다면 더 열심히 공부할 거예요
examples.en: If I became a student again, I would study harder

exercises.prompt: What is the difference between '비가 오면' and '비가 온다면'?
options: 오면 is neutral/likely; 온다면 is more hypothetical
They are exactly the same
오면 is formal; 온다면 is casual
exercises.explanation: -(으)면 is a straightforward 'if' for conditions that may well happen. -(으)ㄴ/는다면 emphasizes the hypothetical nature — 'supposing that' or 'in the event that.'

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'If I were rich' using -(으)ㄴ/는다면?
options: 부자라면
부자다면
부자이면
exercises.explanation: 부자 is a noun, so it takes 이다. The hypothetical form of 이다 is -(이)라면: 부자라면 (if [I] were rich).

exercises.prompt: '그때 더 열심히 공부했다면 좋았을 텐데' means:
options: If I had studied harder back then, it would have been good
If I study harder, it will be good
When I studied harder, it was good
exercises.explanation: -았/었다면 is the past hypothetical: 'if [something] had happened.' Combined with -았/었을 텐데, it expresses regret about an unrealized past condition.

sections.title: Counterfactual Past: -았/었더라면
sections.explanation: -았/었더라면 expresses a counterfactual condition in the past — something that did NOT happen but you are imagining what would have been different if it had. It is the Korean equivalent of 'if only I had...' or 'had I done...' The -더- element adds a reflective, recollective nuance. 일찍 출발했더라면 늦지 않았을 거예요 (If I had left early, I wouldn't have been late). 그 사람을 만났더라면 인생이 달라졌을 거예요 (If I had met that person, my life would have been different). The result clause typically uses -았/었을 거예요 or -았/었을 텐데 to express the imagined outcome. This pattern inherently carries regret or wistful reflection.
sections.tip: -았/었더라면 is ALWAYS about the past and ALWAYS counterfactual (it didn't happen). If you want a condition that might still happen, use -(으)면 or -(으)ㄴ/는다면 instead.

examples.ko: 일찍 일어났더라면 비행기를 놓치지 않았을 거예요
examples.en: If I had woken up early, I wouldn't have missed the plane
If I had confessed back then, what would have happened?

examples.ko: 더 열심히 연습했더라면 시합에서 이겼을 텐데
examples.en: If I had practiced harder, I would have won the match
If I had applied to that company, I might have been accepted

options: Past situations that did NOT happen (counterfactual)
Past situations that DID happen
Future possibilities
exercises.explanation: -았/었더라면 is strictly counterfactual past: it refers to something that did not happen, and imagines what the outcome would have been.

exercises.prompt: '우산을 가져왔더라면 비를 안 맞았을 텐데' means:
options: If I had brought an umbrella, I wouldn't have gotten rained on
If I bring an umbrella, I won't get rained on
I brought an umbrella so I didn't get rained on
exercises.explanation: 가져왔더라면 (if I had brought) is counterfactual — the speaker did NOT bring an umbrella and got rained on, and is expressing regret.

exercises.prompt: Which result clause best pairs with -았/었더라면?
options: -았/었을 거예요 or -았/었을 텐데
-아/어요
-(으)ㄹ 거예요
exercises.explanation: Since -았/었더라면 is counterfactual past, the result should also be in the unrealized past: -았/었을 거예요 (would have) or -았/었을 텐데 (would have been the case that).

sections.title: Regret & Unrealized Expectation: -(으)ㄹ 텐데
sections.explanation: -(으)ㄹ 텐데 expresses an unrealized expectation, assumption, or regret. 텐데 comes from 터 (bound noun meaning 'fact/situation') + 이다 + -ㄴ데. It means 'it would be the case that...' or 'I expect/assume that...' Present/future: 좋을 텐데 (it would be nice / I assume it's nice). 힘들 텐데 (it must be hard). Past counterfactual: 좋았을 텐데 (it would have been nice). 갔을 텐데 (they probably would have gone). When -(으)ㄹ 텐데 ends the sentence, it implies 'but (unfortunately it isn't so)' — expressing regret or sympathy. 여행을 갈 수 있으면 좋을 텐데 (It would be nice if I could travel — but I can't). 많이 피곤할 텐데 쉬세요 (You must be very tired, so please rest — expressing sympathy).
sections.tip: -(으)ㄹ 텐데 at the end of a sentence = 'it would be nice / I assume so, but...' (trailing regret). -(으)ㄹ 텐데 followed by another clause = 'since it's probably the case that...' (expressing assumption and giving advice).

examples.en: It would be nice to have time (but I don't)
You must be hungry — please eat this
They would be happy if they heard that news
It would have been nice if you had told me earlier

options: A wish — 'It would be nice if I spoke Korean well (but I don't)'
A fact — 'I speak Korean well'
Advice — 'You should speak Korean well'
exercises.explanation: 좋을 텐데 at the end of a sentence expresses a wish or regret about something that isn't the case. The speaker wishes they spoke Korean well but currently doesn't.

options: It's probably very cold, so dress warmly
It was very cold, so I dressed warmly
If it's cold, I'll dress warmly
exercises.explanation: -(으)ㄹ 텐데 here expresses an assumption about the listener's situation (it must be cold), followed by advice (dress warmly). This is a sympathetic/caring usage.

exercises.prompt: What does -았/었을 텐데 at the end of a sentence convey?
options: Regret about a past outcome that didn't happen
Certainty about what happened
A plan for the future
exercises.explanation: -았/었을 텐데 expresses regret about an unrealized past: 'it would have been the case that...' It implies things didn't turn out that way, and the speaker wishes they had.
